title: Present simple: I/you
question: 'I ___ coffee every morning.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I/you/we/they, use the base verb: 'I drink.' No -s at the end.

title: Present simple: he/she
question: 'She ___ to school every day.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, add -s or -es: 'She goes.' The verb 'go' becomes 'goes'.

title: Present simple: habit
question: 'We ___ TV in the evening.' Which is correct?
explanation: With we/they, use the base verb: 'We watch.' No -s because the subject is 'we'.

title: Present simple: he reads
question: 'He ___ a book every night.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it in present simple, add -s: 'He reads.'

title: Present simple: they
question: 'They ___ in a big house.' Which is correct?
explanation: With they, use the base verb: 'They live.' No -s with they.

title: Present simple: she works
question: 'My sister ___ in a hospital.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, add -s: 'My sister works.' Sister = she.

title: Present simple: negative I
question: 'I ___ like spicy food.' Which is correct?
explanation: Negative with I/you/we/they: 'don't' + base verb. 'I don't like.'

title: Present simple: negative she
question: 'She ___ eat meat.' Which is correct?
explanation: Negative with he/she/it: 'doesn't' + base verb. 'She doesn't eat.'

title: Present simple: question
question: '___ you speak English?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with I/you/we/they: 'Do' + subject + base verb. 'Do you speak?'

title: Present simple: question he
question: '___ he play football?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with he/she/it: 'Does' + subject + base verb. 'Does he play?'

title: Present continuous: I
question: 'I ___ a letter right now.' Which is correct?
explanation: Present continuous = am/is/are + verb-ing. 'I am writing' (happening now).

title: Present continuous: she
question: 'She ___ on the phone at the moment.' Which is correct?
explanation: With she, use 'is' + verb-ing: 'She is talking.' It is happening now.

title: Present continuous: they
question: 'They ___ dinner now.' Which is correct?
explanation: With they, use 'are' + verb-ing: 'They are cooking.' Happening right now.

title: Present continuous: we
question: 'We ___ for the bus right now.' Which is correct?
explanation: With we, use 'are' + verb-ing: 'We are waiting.' This is happening now.

title: Present continuous: negative
question: 'He ___ sleeping. He is awake.' Which is correct?
explanation: Negative present continuous: am/is/are + not + verb-ing. 'He isn't sleeping.'

title: Present continuous: question
question: '___ you working today?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions in present continuous: Am/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing. 'Are you working?'

title: Be: I am
question: 'I ___ a student.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I, always use 'am': 'I am a student.'

title: Be: he/she/it
question: 'The cat ___ on the table.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it (and singular nouns), use 'is': 'The cat is on the table.'

title: Be: we/they
question: 'My parents ___ at home.' Which is correct?
explanation: With we/you/they (and plural nouns), use 'are': 'My parents are at home.'

title: Be: negative
question: 'I ___ tired today.' (negative) Which is correct?
explanation: Negative of 'be': subject + am/is/are + not. 'I am not tired.'

title: Be: question
question: '___ she your teacher?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with 'be': put am/is/are before the subject. 'Is she your teacher?'

title: Have: I/you
question: 'I ___ two brothers.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I/you/we/they, use 'have': 'I have two brothers.'

title: Have: he/she
question: 'He ___ a red car.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, use 'has': 'He has a red car.'

title: Have: negative
question: 'We ___ a garden.' (negative) Which is correct?
explanation: Negative: 'don't have' with I/you/we/they. 'We don't have a garden.'

title: Have: question
question: '___ you have a pen?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with I/you/we/they: 'Do you have...?' Use 'do' before the subject.

title: Do: emphasis
question: 'I ___ want to go. Really!' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Do' before a base verb adds emphasis with I/you/we/they: 'I do want to go!'

title: Articles: a vs an
question: 'She is ___ engineer.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'an' before a vowel sound: 'an engineer.' The word starts with an 'e' sound.

title: Articles: a
question: 'He has ___ big dog.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'a' before a consonant sound: 'a big dog.' The word 'big' starts with a 'b' sound.

title: Articles: the
question: 'I can see ___ moon tonight.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'the' for unique things that everyone knows: 'the moon.' There is only one moon.

title: Articles: no article
question: 'I like ___. I listen every day.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Music' is uncountable: no 'a/an' and no plural -s. Say 'I like music.'

title: Articles: an + vowel
question: 'I want ___ orange.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'an' before a vowel sound. 'Orange' starts with an 'o' sound, so we say 'an orange.'

title: Articles: the specific
question: '___ sun is very bright today.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'the' for things that are unique or that everyone knows: 'the sun.' There is only one.

title: Plurals: regular -s
question: 'I have three ___.' Which is correct?
explanation: Most nouns add -s for plural: one cat, three cats. Just add -s.

title: Plurals: -es
question: 'I have two ___ on my desk.' Which is correct?
explanation: Nouns ending in -sh, -ch, -s, -x add -es: one box, two boxes.

title: Plurals: -ies
question: 'There are many ___ in the park.' Which is correct?
explanation: Nouns ending in consonant + y: change y to i, add -es. Family becomes families.

title: Plurals: irregular
question: 'The ___ are playing outside.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Child' has an irregular plural: 'children.' You must memorize irregular plurals.

title: Plurals: irregular men
question: 'The ___ are working.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Man' has an irregular plural: 'men.' Not 'mans.'

title: Plurals: no change
question: 'I saw three ___ in the field.' Which is correct?
explanation: Some nouns do not change in plural: one sheep, three sheep.

title: Negation: don't
question: 'We ___ know the answer.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I/you/we/they + action verb, use 'don't': 'We don't know.'

title: Negation: doesn't
question: 'It ___ work. It is broken.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it + action verb, use 'doesn't': 'It doesn't work.'

title: Questions: do/does
question: 'Where ___ they live?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with they + action verb: use 'do.' 'Where do they live?'

title: Questions: does
question: 'What ___ she want?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions with he/she/it + action verb: use 'does.' 'What does she want?'

title: Subject pronouns: he
question: 'My father is a doctor. ___ works at a hospital.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'he' as the subject for a male person: 'He works.' 'He' replaces 'my father.'

title: Subject pronouns: they
question: 'The students are here. ___ are ready.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'they' as the subject for plural people or things: 'They are ready.'

title: Subject pronouns: it
question: 'The book is on the table. ___ is very good.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'it' as the subject for a thing: 'It is very good.' 'It' replaces 'the book.'

title: Subject pronouns: we
question: 'My friend and I like football. ___ play every week.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'we' when you include yourself in a group: 'We play.' 'We' = my friend and I.

title: There is: singular
question: '___ a park near my house.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'there is' with singular nouns: 'There is a park.' One park = singular.

title: There are: plural
question: '___ many people in the room.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'there are' with plural nouns: 'There are many people.' Many = plural.

title: There is: negative
question: '___ no milk in the fridge.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'there is' + no + singular/uncountable noun: 'There is no milk.' Milk = uncountable.

title: There are: question
question: '___ any chairs in the room?' Which is correct?
explanation: Questions: 'Are there' + plural noun. 'Are there any chairs?' Chairs = plural.
